original_abstract: Atom trapping leads to catalysts with atomically dispersed Ru1O5 sites on (100) facets of ceria, as identified by spectroscopy and DFT calculations. This is a new class of ceria-based materials with Ru properties drastically different from the known M/ceria materials. They show excellent activity in catalytic NO oxidation, a critical step that requires use of large loadings of expensive noble metals in diesel aftertreatment systems. Ru1/CeO2 is stable during continuous cycling, ramping, and cooling as well as the presence of moisture. Furthermore, Ru1/CeO2 shows very high NOx storage properties due to formation of stable Ru-NO complexes as well as a high spill-over rate of NOx onto CeO2. Only ∼0.05 wt % of Ru is required for excellent NOx storage. Ru1O5 sites exhibit much higher stability during calcination in air/steam up to 750 °C in contrast to RuO2 nanoparticles. We clarify the location of Ru(II) ions on the ceria surface and experimentally identify the mechanism of NO storage and oxidation using DFT calculations and in situ DRIFTS/mass spectroscopy. Moreover, we show excellent reactivity of Ru1/CeO2 for NO reduction by CO at low temperatures: only 0.1-0.5 wt % of Ru is sufficient to achieve high activity. Modulation-excitation in situ infrared and XPS measurements reveal the individual elementary steps of NO reduction by CO on an atomically dispersed Ru ceria catalyst, highlighting unique properties of Ru1/CeO2 and its propensity to form oxygen vacancies/Ce+3 sites that are critical for NO reduction, even at low Ru loadings. Our study highlights the applicability of novel ceria-based single-atom catalysts to NO and CO abatement.
humanized_abstract: We demonstrated an atom trapping strategy to obtain highly active and stable catalysts, with atomically dispersed Ru1O5 sites on the (100) facets of ceria. By combining spectroscopy and DFT calculations we unambiguously demonstrate the presence of atomically dispersed Ru1O5 species on the ceria surface and demonstrate that they are quite different than the well known M/ceria systems. Moreover, their applications to catalytic NO oxidation is thoroughly investigated and their unique properties and excellent performance demonstrated in great details. We demonstrated that Ru1/CeO2 has a high activity and selectivity in catalytic NO oxidation and is very stable under the continuous cycling conditions as well as under the ramping and cooling procedures and under the moisture. The catalytic NO storage and oxidation properties of Ru1O5 dispersed on the ceria surface are investigated using temperature programmed techniques. The high NOx storage activity of the catalyst is attributed to the stable Ru-NO complexes as well as the high NOx spill-over on CeO2. It is shown that less than 0.05 wt % Ru1O5/CeO2 is required to attain the maximum storage capacity. We also demonstrated that the Ru1O5 sites on the ceria surface have much higher stability during calcination in air/steam at up to 750 °C in comparison to the nanoparticles of RuO2. The DFT, in situ DRIFTS and mass spectroscopy are combined to clarify the location of Ru(II) ions on the ceria surface and the NO storage and oxidation mechanism. Finally, we showed that our atomically dispersed Ru1O5 sites are highly reactive towards CO at low temperatures and a Ru loading of 0.1-0.5 wt % is sufficient to achieve high catalytic activity and light-off temperature. In-situ Modulation-Excitation (ME) infrared spectroscopy and XPS were employed to investigate the individual elementary steps of the reaction of CO with NO over an atomically dispersed Ru ceria catalyst. The excellent reactivity of Ru1/CeO2 catalyst towards NO-CO reaction at low temperatures and even at low Ru loading is demonstrated and the unique properties of this catalyst explained. Our findings demonstrate the high potential of ceria-based single-atom catalysts for automotive catalytic applications.
